agent1_role: M. Alvarez, Marketing Director, GreenGlow Solar
agent2_role: R. Chen, Program Coordinator, Metro City Sustainability Fair
generic:
The Metro City Department of Environment is preparing its annual Sustainability Fair, a major public event celebrating eco-friendly businesses, renewable energy projects, and community-driven green initiatives. The Fair is an important opportunity for businesses to showcase their environmental commitment and for the city to raise awareness around sustainability. Companies that wish to participate purchase display booths along the fairground pathways, as well as limited exhibit spaces inside the central Green Pavilion, where keynote speakers and demonstrations draw the largest audiences. Local news outlets film many of the events inside the Pavilion, making placement there especially desirable for corporate visibility. Each participating company negotiates both the total number of booths they will sponsor throughout the fairgrounds and the number of booth displays they will be allowed inside the Pavilion. The booth sponsorship cost is fixed at $200 per booth, and this rate cannot be negotiated. The Sustainability Fair organizers seek to distribute Pavilion display space as fairly as possible while ensuring companies contribute significantly by sponsoring booths outside the Pavilion. Today, R. Chen is meeting with M. Alvarez from GreenGlow Solar, a regional solar panel manufacturer headquartered in Metro City. Alvarez, representing GreenGlow Solar, wants to maximize their company’s exposure inside the Pavilion, since that is where most attendees and media gather. At the same time, GreenGlow has limited marketing funds this year and needs to balance its desire for high-profile placement with financial responsibility. For Alvarez, success in the negotiation means securing as many Pavilion spots as possible while keeping the total spending lower than the company’s maximum budget.  

Chen, as Program Coordinator, wants to secure a strong overall commitment from GreenGlow in terms of booth sponsorships, since total booth numbers translate directly into revenue for the Sustainability Fair. However, Chen must also carefully ration Pavilion space among multiple sponsors—especially the companies that have been long-term partners or have contributed consistently to the Fair and other city sustainability events. This means Chen will try to limit how many Pavilion booths each individual company receives, leaving room for other sponsors.  

The negotiation centers on determining both the total number of booth sponsorships GreenGlow will buy and how many of those will be located in the Green Pavilion. Chen aims to push for a larger outside-booth commitment, while Alvarez is looking for fewer total spending obligations but maximum Pavilion presence.
agent1_specific:
You are M. Alvarez, Marketing Director for GreenGlow Solar, and you are entering this negotiation with mixed feelings. The Sustainability Fair is always a vital event for your company’s image because it highlights renewable solutions in front of a broad audience, but you are concerned about how much money you can allocate this year. Your CEO has emphasized restraint in marketing budgets, given rising production costs and a series of recent research investments. That means you cannot afford to overspend at the Fair, even though the leadership also wants strong public visibility to compete with rival firms like SunPeak Energy and BrightWave Renewables, who are also likely sponsors.  

Last year, GreenGlow invested in 28 booth sponsorships and managed to secure 8 spots inside the Pavilion. While the arrangement raised the company’s profile, the CEO still complained that competitors were far more visible inside the Pavilion with roughly the same or slightly larger financial commitments. This year, your budget ceiling is set at $12,000 for the Sustainability Fair. With booth sponsorships priced at $200 each, that puts your maximum possible number of booths at 60. On the financial side, you know that while you could theoretically spend up to $12,000, you would rather spend less if possible and reserve funds for other marketing opportunities later in the year. Your CEO is especially keen on community-focused events and digital campaigns that highlight customer engagement, rather than simply spending heavily at one high-profile fair. That said, if GreenGlow shows up at the Sustainability Fair with a low presence—particularly inside the Green Pavilion—your CEO will likely question your judgment and compare your performance unfavorably to competitors. Thus, you face a dilemma: you need enough Pavilion presence to satisfy leadership, but you do not want to overshoot the budget or waste resources on too many booths scattered outside the central area.  

To prepare, you have set up a scoring system to help clarify what outcomes work for you. Since Pavilion presence is more important for visibility, you give far more weight to Pavilion spots than to the overall number of booths. At the same time, you assign a penalty to yourself for overspending beyond your CEO’s preferred range of 40 to 50 total booths, since going too high will sacrifice funds for other initiatives later in the season. To keep yourself disciplined during the negotiation, here is the scoring system you will apply: For each Pavilion booth that GreenGlow secures, you will ADD 6 points to your score. For every booth beyond 50 that you agree to buy overall, you will SUBTRACT 4 points from your score. This reflects your view that increased spending above the budget’s comfort zone is costly and undesirable. On the other hand, filling Pavilion space substantially boosts visibility and is highly valued.  

You have also established a bottom line for yourself: you will not accept any agreement that gives you fewer than 40 points. This ensures that the deal provides enough Pavilion visibility relative to the cost.  

Here’s an example of how your scoring works: If you purchase 55 booths in total and manage to secure 18 Pavilion spots, your score would be 88. You earn 108 points from the Pavilion presence (18 × 6 = 108), but you must subtract 20 points for going 5 booths beyond 50 (5 × 4 = 20). The resulting score is 88, which comfortably surpasses your threshold.
agent2_specific:
You are eager to ensure that GreenGlow Solar makes a significant booth commitment at this year’s Sustainability Fair. Although GreenGlow has participated before, their contributions have diminished over the past few years, and their representative, M. Alvarez, has consistently argued for more high-profile placement in the Pavilion while scaling back their overall sponsorship. You recall that last year’s conversations became tense, with Alvarez accusing the Fair of “playing favorites” with larger companies that received more central Pavilion exposure.  

Your concern is that Pavilion space is extremely limited, but nearly every major sponsor considers it the most valuable spot. The city relies on the Pavilion for maximum visibility since media crews focus their coverage there. You must balance these demands while maintaining a sense of fairness and rewarding the most supportive, long-term partners of the Fair. GreenGlow does not fall into that category in your eyes: while they are a recognizable local company, they do not consistently sponsor additional environmental initiatives in Metro City the way some competitors do.  

Therefore, although you want to secure their financial support, you want to avoid allocating disproportionate Pavilion spots to GreenGlow. Alvarez tends to negotiate aggressively, so you are preparing to remain firm. In planning your negotiation strategy, you have set a strict structural guideline: no company may receive more than one-third of its total booth sponsorships inside the Green Pavilion. This rule was introduced to reduce complaints from sponsors about unfair advantages and to keep Pavilion exposure balanced across participants. Within that hard cap, your personal preference is to assign GreenGlow no more than 5 Pavilion spots, given their inconsistent support in recent years—and your scoring system reflects this preference by penalizing allocations beyond 5. You know Alvarez will push hard for Pavilion spots, but it is imperative to remind them that these premium locations are extremely limited, and many companies are competing for the same visibility.  

Last year GreenGlow bought 28 booths, 8 of which were in the Pavilion. For 2024, you are expected to increase overall revenue, so you want GreenGlow to commit to at least 30 booths. At $200 per booth, that would be $6,000 in revenue. Ideally, you would like them to contribute even more, because Metro City is counting on a strong fundraising year to continue investing in renewable energy awareness and community sustainability programs.  

While Pavilion exposure will likely dominate Alvarez’s concerns, your priority is to maximize external booth purchases—the bread and butter of the Fair’s financial success. To strengthen your position, you have built a scoring system that reflects your priorities. You care far more about the total number of booths GreenGlow sponsors than about how many they receive in the Pavilion. Your system works as follows: for each booth above 25 that GreenGlow purchases, **ADD 6 points** to your score. For each Pavilion booth they are allowed beyond 5, **SUBTRACT 4 points** from your score. This reflects your desire to maximize revenue through larger commitments while discouraging an excessive Pavilion allocation.  

You have determined that you will not accept any deal worth fewer than **60 points**. Since other companies are waiting to negotiate, you cannot afford to waste time accepting a low-value agreement with GreenGlow. You should press Alvarez toward sponsoring well beyond the 30-booth minimum; at the same time, carefully limit how Pavilion spots are distributed so as not to upset the broader sponsor pool.  

Here’s an example to guide you: if Alvarez agrees to 40 booths total, with 10 of them in the Pavilion, your score equals 70. Each booth above 25 yields 6 points; 40 is 15 above 25, giving you 90 points. Each Pavilion booth beyond 5 costs 4 points; 10 Pavilion spots is 5 beyond the threshold, so you subtract 20 points (5 × 4 = 20). The resulting score is 90 − 20 = 70, which falls above your reservation threshold of 60.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did M. Alvarez and R. Chen reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if M. Alvarez and R. Chen did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.
3. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for M. Alvarez? Answer with a number.
4. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for R. Chen? Answer with a number.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           how many Pavilion booths did GreenGlow secure?           Answer with a number or N/A only."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))          and min(1, int(m.group()) * 6 / 120)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many total booths did GreenGlow agree to purchase?          Answer with a number or N/A only."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and max(0, 1 - (max(0, int(m.group()) - 50) * 4) / 40)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         what was GreenGlow's final score according to M. Alvarez’s scoring system?          Answer with a number or N/A only."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"-?\d+", val))         and min(1, max(0, int(m.group())) / 120 if int(m.group()) >= 40 else 0)     ) or 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many total booths did GreenGlow commit to purchasing?          Answer with a number between 0-60 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and min(1, max(0, (float(m.group()) - 25) * 6) / 210)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many Pavilion booths did GreenGlow receive?          Answer with a number between 0-20 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, 1 - max(0, (float(m.group()) - 5) * 4) / 60)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was R. Chen's final score according to the Program Coordinator’s scoring system?          Answer with a number or N/A only."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"-?\d+", val))         and min(1, max(0, int(m.group())) / 210 if int(m.group()) >= 60 else 0)     ) or 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did M. Alvarez, Marketing Director, GreenGlow Solar and R. Chen, Program Coordinator, Metro City Sustainability Fair reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to strengthen participants’ ability to conduct careful pre-negotiation analysis, particularly by distinguishing between their aspirations and their best alternative to a negotiated agreement (BATNA). The exercise encourages learners to compare distributive and integrative approaches, recognizing when value-creating trades can be made by focusing on issues that each side values differently. Along the way, participants gain practice with a wide range of negotiation dynamics, such as how to effectively set anchors, manage reservation prices, and use or respond to pressure tactics. They will also explore how bluffing, misrepresentation, and public opinion can shape bargaining behavior, while analyzing the role of constituents and the challenges posed by monolithic versus non-monolithic parties. Additional themes include assessing offers and counteroffers, grappling with precedents, linking issues strategically, and thinking creatively about options through techniques such as dovetailing interests. Participants will also be encouraged to reflect on what “success” means in a negotiation and to understand the implications of closure, choice perception, and tactical sequencing in building durable agreements.
categories: Anchoring, BATNA, Criteria, Emotions, Identity and Core Values, Intra-Organizational Conflict, Joint Gains, Power Dynamics, Principal-Agent Dynamics, Relationships, ZOPA
title: Pavilion Prestige vs. Booth Commitment: The GreenGlow–Metro City Fair Negotiation
oneline: A negotiation between GreenGlow Solar and the Metro City Sustainability Fair to balance GreenGlow’s desire for maximum Pavilion exposure with the Fair’s goal of securing greater overall booth sponsorships.
